squiz-plan · clipsi — self-hostable URL shortener with analytics
plan

clipsi — self-hostable URL shortener with analytics

Six load-bearing sections for a tiny but real service: redirect path, analytics pipeline, four embedded engineering decisions. Designed to fit in one binary, one config file, one $5 VPS.

section notes

Mission

OVR-1

A self-hostable URL shortener that small teams can stand up on their own domain in an afternoon. Bring-your-own-domain, real analytics, no third party tracking your users.

feedback

Audience — who comes first?

OVR-2

We can frame v1 around different primary users. The choice cascades into FR scope, default config, and dashboard density. Pick one to optimise FOR; the others still work, just second-class.

feedback

Hard constraints

OVR-3

One static binary. One config file. Must run on a $5/month VPS with 1 GB RAM. No SaaS dependencies for the redirect path — even if every other service is down, slugs still resolve.

feedback
section notes

Create a short link

FR-1

POST /api/links with a target URL → returns a slug. Web form mirrors the API. Auth is optional per-instance (open / api-key / OAuth).

  • refs
  • OVR-1
feedback

Redirect by slug

FR-2

GET /:slug → HTTP 302 to the stored target. The hot path of the service: cheap, cacheable, no auth, no DB write.

  • refs
  • OVR-1
  • OVR-2
feedback

Custom slugs + auto-generation

FR-3

User can specify the slug ("black-friday-2026") or let the server generate a short base62 ID. Custom slugs collide-check; auto slugs are 6-8 chars.

  • refs
  • FR-1
feedback

Link expiry (optional)

FR-4

Per-link TTL: "expires in 30 days" or "after 1000 clicks". A nightly job sweeps expired links. Expired slugs return HTTP 410 Gone.

  • refs
  • FR-1
feedback

Per-link analytics (no PII)

FR-5

Per-slug dashboard: total clicks, referrers, rough geo (country only), user agent buckets. IPs are hashed; no cookies; no individual user records — only aggregates.

  • refs
  • OVR-1
feedback
section notes

Redirect p99 < 50 ms

NFR-1

From the moment a browser sends GET /:slug to the moment it gets a 302 response — under 50 ms at the 99th percentile, measured from the same region as the VPS.

  • refs
  • OVR-1
feedback

Survives reboot + buffered writes

NFR-2

All link state in durable storage before any 200 is returned to the creator. Analytics events are buffered locally and replayed if the analytics backend is briefly unreachable.

  • refs
  • OVR-3
feedback

No PII storage

NFR-3

IP addresses are SHA-256 hashed with a per-instance salt before any write. No cookies on the redirect path. No third party scripts ever in the dashboard.

  • refs
  • OVR-1
feedback

One binary, one config

NFR-4

Single static Go binary plus one TOML config file. No external dependencies for the redirect path (cache is in-process by default; external cache is opt-in).

  • refs
  • OVR-3
feedback
section notes

"Our newsletter went viral"

CASE-1

Marketing sends an email with one tracked link. 10 000 req/s for 90 seconds. The redirect path must hold; the analytics pipeline can fall behind and catch up — but no redirect can drop.

  • refs
  • FR-2
  • NFR-1
feedback

"I want a memorable slug for the all-hands"

CASE-2

Someone types a slug into the form ("all-hands-q4"), pastes the Zoom link, copies the resulting short URL, and pastes it into the calendar invite. End-to-end under 30 seconds, no API key.

  • refs
  • FR-1
  • FR-3
feedback

"We printed it on a flyer"

CASE-3

A short URL was printed on 5 000 flyers six months ago. The slug must still resolve. Expiry never set; data never lost; the link works as long as the box is up.

  • refs
  • FR-2
  • NFR-2
feedback

Primary scenario to optimise for

CASE-priority

All three cases above are real, but they pull in slightly different directions (CASE-1 wants raw throughput; CASE-2 wants UX speed; CASE-3 wants permanence). v1 can serve them all decently but should be tuned for one. Which?

  • refs
  • CASE-1
  • CASE-2
  • CASE-3
feedback
section notes

HTTP API service

ENG-1

Single Go process: net/http, no framework. Routes: GET /:slug (hot path), POST /api/links, GET /api/links/:slug, GET /api/links/:slug/stats, plus a tiny dashboard SPA served from go:embed.

  • refs
  • FR-1
  • FR-2
  • FR-3
  • NFR-1
feedback

Link storage backend

ENG-2

Where the slug → target map lives. The hot path issues one read per redirect; the cold path writes one row per created link.

  • refs
  • FR-1
  • FR-3
  • FR-4
  • NFR-2
feedback

Redirect cache strategy

ENG-3

How the hot path stays under 50 ms p99 when a popular link gets hammered. The cache is read-mostly: invalidate on update; rebuild on miss.

  • refs
  • FR-2
  • NFR-1
feedback

Analytics pipeline

ENG-4

Click events flow from the redirect handler into an event log, get aggregated nightly into per-slug stats. PII stripped at the handler before the event is even written.

  • refs
  • FR-5
  • NFR-3
feedback

Deploy target

ENG-5

How the binary actually runs in production. All three options should work from the same artifact — the binary is platform-aware.

  • refs
  • NFR-4
feedback
section notes

API scaffolding + slug generation

BUILD-1

Stand up the HTTP server, define the routes, implement base62 ID generation, wire collision-check for custom slugs. Auth modes (open / api-key / OAuth) gated behind config.

  • refs
  • ENG-1
  • ENG-2
feedback

Hot-path redirect

BUILD-2

GET /:slug → cache lookup → DB fallback → 302. The most-trafficked code path in the service; budget < 5 ms server-side on a cache hit.

  • refs
  • ENG-1
  • ENG-3
  • NFR-1
feedback

Dashboard + create-link UI

BUILD-3

Tiny embedded HTML/JS dashboard: list links, create, see per-slug stats. Served from go:embed, no separate web server. Tailwind-flavored CSS in one stylesheet.

  • refs
  • ENG-1
  • FR-1
  • FR-5
feedback

Analytics ingestion + nightly rollup

BUILD-4

Handler emits click event → buffered queue → worker writes to events table. Nightly cron rolls events into per-slug daily aggregates the dashboard reads.

  • refs
  • ENG-1
  • ENG-4
  • FR-5
feedback

Deploy guide + handover

BUILD-5

One-page deploy script per target (systemd / docker / k8s). Worked example: from `wget` to first redirect in under 5 minutes. Backup + restore documented.

  • refs
  • ENG-5
feedback

Ship sequence — order to ship the 5 builds in

BUILD-sequence

BUILD-1..5 are roughly equal in size; the order changes what the user sees first and how usable each intermediate cut is.

  • refs
  • BUILD-1
  • BUILD-2
  • BUILD-3
  • BUILD-4
  • BUILD-5
feedback
00 / 00 items touched
